content:
The **likelihood** idea grew from turning **probability models** into **inference engines**. **Bernoulli** and **Laplace** used products of success probabilities to assess coin biases (early 1800s). **Fisher** (1912–1922) formalized **likelihood** as a distinct concept from **probability**, introduced **maximum likelihood estimation**, and developed the **score** and **Fisher information** as derivatives of log-likelihood.

**Neyman** and **Pearson** linked likelihood ratios to hypothesis tests; **Bayesian** statisticians (**Jeffreys**, later **Savage**) placed the same L(θ) inside **Bayes' theorem** as the data term. **Cox** (1950s) articulated the **likelihood principle**: evidence about θ should enter only through L(θ) for observed data.

Machine learning inherited this through **logistic regression** (Bernoulli log-likelihood), **GLMs** (exponential-family log-likelihoods), and modern **cross-entropy** losses—each is negative log-likelihood under an explicit f(x | θ).

timeline.label: Bernoulli / Laplace (1800s)
timeline.description: Products of trial probabilities used to infer unknown success rates from observed outcomes.

timeline.label: Fisher formalizes likelihood (1912–1922)
timeline.description: L(θ) distinguished from P(θ | x); MLE and information theory of inference born.

timeline.label: Neyman–Pearson & likelihood ratio tests (1930s)
timeline.description: Hypothesis testing framed via comparing maximized likelihoods under competing models.

timeline.label: ML losses as log-likelihood (1900s–today)
timeline.description: GLMs and neural nets train by minimizing negative log-likelihood—cross-entropy is the modern face of Categorical/Bernoulli L(θ).
